metadata.title: The Company Concentration Nobody’s Talking About
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The Lever data comes from one of the two major ATS platforms venture-backed companies use. A few companies dominate: Shield AI alone posts 389 jobs, 27.8% of the entire dataset. Role-repetition patterns reveal who is desperately staffing up versus carefully selecting. Even missing metadata, like 'Remote' with no country, becomes a useful analytical signal.

artifactLines: The Lever data comes from one of the two major ATS platforms venture-backed companies use.
A few companies dominate: Shield AI alone posts 389 jobs, 27.8% of the entire dataset.
Role-repetition patterns reveal who is desperately staffing up versus carefully selecting.
Even missing metadata, like 'Remote' with no country, becomes a useful analytical signal.

beats.narration_text: The concentration jumped out: a handful of companies own most of the 1,400 jobs in the Lever data. I want to mine that pattern myself. Show me: (1) why Shield AI's 389 postings, 27.8% of the dataset, signals desperate staffing rather than healthy hiring, (2) how role-repetition patterns distinguish companies scaling frantically from ones selecting carefully, and (3) how the missing metadata, like 'Remote' with no country, can still be turned into a useful signal instead of noise.

output: When metadata disappears, what remains is the hiring pattern at i
You can’t track hiring velocity week-over-week without timestamps
